eviction.court_process_summary_rule: {"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A; M.G.L. c. 186, § 18","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"14-day notice to quit for nonpayment (c. 239, § 8A); summary process action follows","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to quit for month-to-month tenancy (or longer if lease specifies); 7-day notice for week-to-week tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice to cure or quit for material breach","no_cause":"30-day notice to terminate month-to-month tenancy (statewide); Boston requires just cause for eviction of covered units"},"full_text":"M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A. Summary process for nonpayment of rent. (a) A landlord may bring a summary process action for nonpayment of rent if the rent is 7 days or more overdue. The landlord must first give the tenant a 14-day notice to quit. (b) The court shall grant judgment for the landlord if the tenant fails to pay all overdue rent and reasonable costs within 7 days after the return day. (c) If the tenant pays all overdue rent and costs before judgment, the action shall be dismissed.\nM.G.L. c. 186, § 18. Retaliatory eviction. No landlord may evict or attempt to evict a tenant solely because the tenant has exercised any right under this chapter or any other law. If the landlord brings an eviction action within 6 months of the tenant's protected activity, there is a rebuttable presumption of retaliation.\nBoston Just Cause Eviction (Local Ordinance, effective 2024): Covered residential units may not be evicted except for just cause, which includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, and substantial rehabilitation."}

{"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 186, §§ 15B, 15A","full_text":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B. Security deposits; interest; termination; landlord's statement of condition.
(1)(a) Security deposit limitation. A landlord may not demand or receive from a tenant a security deposit in an amount exceeding the amount of the first month's rent. The total of all deposits and last month's rent may not exceed the amount of first month's rent plus an amount equal to one-half of the first month's rent.
(1)(b) Separate account; interest. The landlord shall deposit the security deposit in a Massachusetts banking institution in a separate, interest-bearing account in the tenant's name. The landlord shall pay the tenant interest on the security deposit, at the rate determined annually by the Massachusetts Commissioner of Banks (currently 5% per annum or the regular passbook rate, whichever is greater), in yearly installments, or at the termination of tenancy, whichever occurs first. The tenant is entitled to receive the interest earned while residing in the premises.
(2) Landlord's statement of condition. Within 10 days after the tenant commences occupation of the premises, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant a written statement of the condition of the premises. The tenant may supplement or dispute the statement.
(3) Return of security deposit. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions, within 30 days after the tenant vacates the premises. The landlord shall provide an itemized list of any deductions with supporting receipts.
(4) Failure to comply. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 30 days, the tenant may bring an action in the district court or housing court to recover the security deposit and may be awarded damages equal to three times the amount of the security deposit, plus interest and reasonable attorney's fees.
(7) Retaliation. The taking of a security deposit shall not be construed as a waiver of any other rights of the landlord or tenant."}

sources.title:
{"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 186, §§ 15B, 15A","full_text":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 15B. Security deposits; interest; termination; landlord's statement of condition.
(1)(a) Security deposit limitation. A landlord may not demand or receive from a tenant a security deposit in an amount exceeding the amount of the first month's rent. The total of all deposits and last month's rent may not exceed the amount of first month's rent plus an amount equal to one-half of the first month's rent.
(1)(b) Separate account; interest. The landlord shall deposit the security deposit in a Massachusetts banking institution in a separate, interest-bearing account in the tenant's name. The landlord shall pay the tenant interest on the security deposit, at the rate determined annually by the Massachusetts Commissioner of Banks (currently 5% per annum or the regular passbook rate, whichever is greater), in yearly installments, or at the termination of tenancy, whichever occurs first. The tenant is entitled to receive the interest earned while residing in the premises.
(2) Landlord's statement of condition. Within 10 days after the tenant commences occupation of the premises, the landlord shall deliver to the tenant a written statement of the condition of the premises. The tenant may supplement or dispute the statement.
(3) Return of security deposit. Upon termination of the tenancy, the landlord shall return the security deposit to the tenant, less any deductions, within 30 days after the tenant vacates the premises. The landlord shall provide an itemized list of any deductions with supporting receipts.
(4) Failure to comply. If the landlord fails to return the security deposit within 30 days, the tenant may bring an action in the district court or housing court to recover the security deposit and may be awarded damages equal to three times the amount of the security deposit, plus interest and reasonable attorney's fees.
(7) Retaliation. The taking of a security deposit shall not be construed as a waiver of any other rights of the landlord or tenant."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A; M.G.L. c. 186, § 18","notice_requirements":{"nonpayment_of_rent":"14-day notice to quit for nonpayment (c. 239, § 8A); summary process action follows","holdover_tenant":"30-day notice to quit for month-to-month tenancy (or longer if lease specifies); 7-day notice for week-to-week tenancy","lease_violation":"30-day notice to cure or quit for material breach","no_cause":"30-day notice to terminate month-to-month tenancy (statewide); Boston requires just cause for eviction of covered units"},"full_text":"M.G.L. c. 239, § 8A. Summary process for nonpayment of rent. (a) A landlord may bring a summary process action for nonpayment of rent if the rent is 7 days or more overdue. The landlord must first give the tenant a 14-day notice to quit. (b) The court shall grant judgment for the landlord if the tenant fails to pay all overdue rent and reasonable costs within 7 days after the return day. (c) If the tenant pays all overdue rent and costs before judgment, the action shall be dismissed.\nM.G.L. c. 186, § 18. Retaliatory eviction. No landlord may evict or attempt to evict a tenant solely because the tenant has exercised any right under this chapter or any other law. If the landlord brings an eviction action within 6 months of the tenant's protected activity, there is a rebuttable presumption of retaliation.\nBoston Just Cause Eviction (Local Ordinance, effective 2024): Covered residential units may not be evicted except for just cause, which includes nonpayment of rent, material breach, nuisance, illegal activity, owner move-in, and substantial rehabilitation."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 15","full_text":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 15. Right of entry. A landlord may enter a tenant's premises: (1) At reasonable times upon reasonable notice (generally accepted as 24 hours); (2) In case of emergency without notice; (3) To make repairs or improvements; (4) To show the premises to prospective tenants or buyers. The tenant may recover damages for unlawful entry or harassment. Entry without proper notice may constitute a violation of the tenant's rights."}

sources.title: {"statutes":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 14","full_text":"M.G.L. c. 186, § 14. Implied warranty of fitness for habitation. In every written or oral rental agreement for a dwelling unit, there is an implied warranty that the premises are and will remain fit for human habitation. This means the premises must be suitable for occupation in the area where they are located and must include: (1) Effective waterproofing of the roof and exterior walls; (2) Plumbing and gas facilities in good working order; (3) Clean and sanitary conditions; (4) Functioning heating facilities adequate to heat all habitable rooms; (5) Electrical lighting and outlets in good working order; (6) Freedom from vermin and pests; (7) Compliance with state sanitary codes and local health ordinances. This warranty may not be waived in a residential tenancy. If the landlord violates this warranty, the tenant may: (a) Withhold rent; (b) Bring an action for damages; (c) Terminate the lease; (d) In Boston, Cambridge, and some other municipalities, repair and deduct."}
